Tafseer:
Arguing for the Resurrection from the pairing of everything:
An oath is sworn by the night and day, male and female. We repeatedly explain that oaths in the Qur'an serve as testimonies to a claim. The night and day, male and female, share the relationship of pairs (mates), cooperating to fulfill the purpose of their creation. Each contains a void that is filled only by its pair; without it, their existences would have no utility, and their capabilities could not be justified. The Qur'an has used this harmony among opposites to prove monotheism, and also the Resurrection, which is the objective here, as stated in Surah Adh-Dhariyat:
(And of all things We created two mates; perhaps you will remember) (Adh-Dhariyat, 51:49).
That is: remember that this world also has its pair—namely, the Hereafter, which must inevitably manifest so that this world reaches its purpose. This was explained under the cited verse and in other places of this book. We will also throw light on this under the subject of the oaths.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 1 and 2 was combined in the original text.]

In 'And [by] He Who created the male and female' (wa ma khalaqa-dh-dhakara wa-l-untha), 'ma' is used in our view as an infinitive (masdariyah). We discussed this under Surah Ash-Shams, verse 5, and repeating it here would be lengthy.

The earnings of the righteous and the wicked will not be equal:
This is the primary claim proved by the testimonies mentioned above. That is: the pairing of everything in the universe, and everything reaching its purpose through its pair, requires that this world also has its pair—namely, the Hereafter, which must inevitably manifest so that this world reaches its purpose; otherwise, it remains a vain work. If there is no Hereafter, it means the Creator regards the righteous and the wicked as equal. It is contrary to His wisdom to make the effort and earnings of the righteous and the wicked equal.
'Sa'y' means effort, and also means the fruit of effort (earnings). Here, it is used in this second meaning. Precedents exist in the Qur'an, as in:
(And that there is not for man except that [good] for which he strives [sa'a], and that his effort [sa'yahu] is going to be seen) (An-Najm, 53:39-40).
Proving the Resurrection from its necessity: 'Shatta' is the plural of 'shateet', which means diverse—showing that it is a requirement of intellect and nature that the results of the effort of the righteous and the wicked should not be identical, but diverse in accordance with their deeds. Those who earned goodness should receive His favor, and those who earned evil should face its consequence. Thus, the claim of the Resurrection is presented here from its necessity—showing that it must occur because without it, this world is an unjust place, as He asked those who deny it:
(Then did you think that We created you uselessly and that to Us you would not be returned?) (Al-Mu'minun, 23:115),
and elsewhere in amazement:
(Then will We treat the Muslims like the criminals? What is the matter with you? How do you judge?) (Al-Qalam, 68:35-36).

Detail of the difference between the earnings of the righteous and the wicked:
This details the difference that must inevitably manifest between the earnings of the righteous and the wicked. It is stated: as for him who spends in the way of Allah, fears his Lord, and believes in the best recompense, We will ease him toward ease.
Using 'and fears' (wa-ttaqa) after 'gives' (a'ta) shows that this spending should not be for show, but solely to seek His pleasure and out of fear of a Day when only righteous deeds will benefit, as described in Surah Ad-Dahr:
(They fulfill their vows and fear a Day whose evil will be widespread. And they give food in spite of love for it to the needy, the orphan, and the captive, [saying], 'We feed you only for the countenance of Allah. We wish not from you reward or gratitude. Indeed, We fear from our Lord a harsh, distressful Day.') (76:7-10).

'And believes in the best [reward]' (wa saddaqa bi-l-husna) - 'Husna' (the best) is an attribute whose noun 'a-l-'aqibah' (recompense/end) is omitted based on context—meaning they believe in the best recompense of their deeds. This points to the primary motive of their virtues: along with fear of the Hereafter, they also believe that Allah Almighty will fully reward their every virtue.
If a person lacks fear of the Hereafter and does not believe that He will reward his smallest virtue, he cannot find the courage to spend, and if he does, he will do so for some worldly interest, which is completely devoid of blessing. In Surah Al-Ma'un, He states:
(Have you seen the one who denies the Recompense? For that is the one who drives away the orphan, and does not feel the urge to feed the needy) (107:1-3).

'We will ease him toward ease' (fa-sanuyassiruhu lil-yusra) - 'Yusra' (ease) is an attribute whose noun is omitted, just like in 'husna'—meaning: 'the easy recompense/end'. This points to the divine law: if a person chooses the path of goodness, Allah Almighty makes its difficulties easy for him and guides him to success, as He states:
(And those who strive for Us—We will surely guide them to Our ways) (Al-Ankabut, 29:69).
Their end is described as 'yusra' (ease) because their account will be easy, as stated in Surah Al-Inshiqaq:
(Then as for he who is given his record in his right hand, he will be judged with an easy account) (84:7-8).

The end of the opposite group:
This describes the end of the second group—those who regard this worldly life as the only life. They are miserly with their wealth and are completely untroubled by the Hereafter, neither believing in recompense nor possessing the courage to strive for it. It is stated: their end will be completely different from the first group, as Allah Almighty will grant them respite on the path that leads to a highly difficult end. Here too, the noun for 'usra' (difficulty) is omitted, and 'taysir' (easing) is used in the meaning of 'imhal' (granting respite).
This divine law is explained in various places: if a person does not choose the path of goodness, Allah Almighty hands his reins to his desires. Since he faces no struggle against his soul, he assumes his path is smooth. Following his desires, he reaches the end of life, when he must face what is described as:
(I will burden him with a difficult climb) (Al-Muddaththir, 74:17). This is what is pointed to here.
In contrast, those who choose faith and righteous deeds must struggle against their desires at every step, which gives them the strength to break through the difficult passes of the path, until they reach the final destination:
(And enter among My [righteous] servants, and enter My Paradise) (Al-Fajr, 89:29-30).
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 8, 9, and 10 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 8, 9, and 10 was combined in the original text.]

Warning to the misers:
The particle 'ma' here can be negative as well as interrogative. While both are grammatically correct, the interrogative style is more powerful, so I preferred it. This is an interrogative warning and rebuke to those who are miserly: of what use will his wealth be to him when he falls into the pit of Hell! The meaning is: the only permanent benefit of wealth is to spend it in the way of Allah to preserve it for the Hereafter; if he fails to do so, it brings him no benefit, but becomes a source of misery.

This is the same warning in powerful words: Our duty was only to show the path, which We have done by sending Our Messenger and revealing Our Book. It is not Our duty to force people to walk on this path. This is their own responsibility; those who choose it will succeed, and those who turn away will face the end.

'And indeed, to Us belongs the Hereafter and the first [life]' (wa inna lana lal-akhirata wa-l-oola) - This adds to the warning: people should remember that the matters of both the Hereafter and the world are in Our authority alone. No one can achieve anything in this world or the Hereafter on the strength of their own plans or power. If anyone boasts of his family status or deities, let him know that they have no existence in the Hereafter, and everyone will face only Allah, the One without partners.
The delusion of the wealthy misers: Note again: (But as for him who is stingy and considers himself self-sufficient) (verse 8). The wealthy misers fall into the delusion that what they possess is the fruit of their own capabilities; therefore, they become completely independent of God. When Qarun was reminded to recognize God's right in the wealth He bestowed, he responded:
(I was only given it because of knowledge I have) (Al-Qasas, 28:78)
—meaning: 'I acquired it through my own intelligence, so what does God have to do with it!' This is the mindset of every capitalist. Through 'And indeed, to Us belongs the Hereafter and the first', the Qur'an has dealt a blow to this false assumption—showing that what one receives in this world and the Hereafter is granted by Him alone. He has no partner in this world or the Hereafter.

Addressing the Quraysh:
Having stated the reality in a general form, He addresses the Quraysh directly: since warning you before was necessary, I have warned you of a flaming Fire, which only the most wretched (ashqa) will enter—those who denied and turned away. And the most righteous (atqa) will be kept far from it—those who spend their wealth to purify themselves.
Clearing a misunderstanding: Note that the contrast here is not between those of high or low degrees of wretchedness or righteousness, but between those who deny the Messenger and those who accept him. The Messenger established the proof perfectly; therefore, those who reject him are all 'ashqa' (most wretched), as indicated by: (Who denied and turned away), showing that the discussion is about those who rejected the Messenger directly. It is stated: they are the most wretched, and will enter the Fire that is prepared and raging. Conversely, those who took heed and spent their wealth to purify themselves are all 'atqa' (most righteous) because they accepted the call when society was hostile and few had the courage to choose the path of goodness. This is why his early companions hold the status of 'the foremost' (sabiqun) and 'those near' (muqarrabun).
Based on this, some have argued that Hell is reserved only for those of high degrees of wretchedness, and ordinary sinners will not enter, which is incorrect. If we accept this, one could also argue that only those of high degrees of righteousness (atqa) will be saved from Hell, and ordinary believers will not enter, or that only they will enter Paradise, which is baseless. This misunderstanding arose from failing to understand the context of the discourse. We have clarified the context, which clears this misunderstanding.

[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 14, 15, 16, 17, and 18 was combined in the original text.]

Only the spending for His pleasure is accepted:
This explains: (Who gives his wealth to purify himself) (verse 18)—meaning: for the purpose of self-purification, only the spending that is done solely to seek His pleasure is accepted with Allah Almighty, without wanting any reward or gratitude from anyone. We cited the verse from Surah Ad-Dahr:
(We wish not from you reward or gratitude) (76:9).
This same is stated in other words here.
Clearing a misunderstanding: Some have translated this as: 'And no one has any favor on him which he should return.' Although grammatically correct, if it means that if someone spends on a person who previously did him a favor, this spending is not for His pleasure, this is incorrect. Repaying a favor is indeed a virtue, even if it is not of the same high degree as helping someone who did no favor. If a poor person once gave you water, and you feed him when he is hungry, will this not be for His pleasure!
We have adopted a translation that is grammatically correct and is free of any such confusion.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 19 and 20 was combined in the original text.]
